threats.title: Missing Authorization in updateOrder Allows Any User to Modify Any Active Order
threats.hypothesis_description: The updateOrder function in MarginTradingHook does not verify that the order being modified belongs to the caller's position. Unlike cancelOrder which checks `order.initPosId == initPosId`, updateOrder only verifies the caller has any valid position and sufficient collateral. An attacker can modify any victim's active order parameters (triggerPrice, limitPrice, tokenOut, collAmt) and then fill the modified order to steal the victim's collateral.
threats.investigation_rationale: Direct comparison of cancelOrder (has auth check at line ~248) vs updateOrder (missing auth check) reveals the omission. The fillOrder function trusts stored order parameters, making this a complete attack chain.

proof_of_signal.pattern: cancelOrder has `_require(order.initPosId == initPosId, Errors.INVALID_INPUT)` at line ~248 but updateOrder at line ~330 omits this check entirely while modifying order storage

preconditions: Victim has at least one active stop-loss or take-profit order
Attacker has their own valid margin position with non-zero collateral
Attacker knows the victim's orderId (observable on-chain)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Missing ownership check: order.initPosId == initPosId not verified
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: updateOrder reads initPosId from caller's mapping but never asserts the order's initPosId matches; order fields are directly overwritten in storage

analysis_questions: Can the attacker set tokenOut to a valueless ERC20 to minimize the cost of filling?
Does changing limitPrice_e36 allow extracting maximum collateral value?
Can triggerPrice be set to ensure immediate fillability?
validation_requirements: Construct PoC: create attacker position, call updateOrder with victim's orderId, modify limitPrice to extreme value, then call fillOrder
Verify collateral extraction exceeds cost of repaying debt
Confirm no other check prevents cross-position order modification

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Attacker creates their own margin position via openPos. 2) Attacker calls updateOrder(attackerPosId, victimOrderId, ...) setting limitPrice to extreme value favoring the attacker. 3) Attacker calls fillOrder(victimOrderId), paying minimal borrToken repayment while receiving victim's collateral shares worth significantly more.
prerequisites: Attacker has a valid position with non-zero collateral

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete theft of collateral from any user with active margin orders
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users with active stop-loss or take-profit orders
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: No recovery — collateral transferred to attacker via decollateralize

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test comparing updateOrder and cancelOrder authorization paths

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Attacker needs enough tokens to repay proportional debt
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Attack visible on-chain via UpdateOrder event with mismatched initPosId

threats.title: Swapped Return Values in _calculateRepaySize Break All Order Fills After Interest Accrual
threats.hypothesis_description: The _calculateRepaySize function returns (uint repayAmt, uint repayShares) but _calculateFillOrderInfo destructures the return as (repayShares, repayAmt), swapping debt shares and token amounts. After any interest accrues (shares ≠ amounts), fillOrder attempts to repay using the token amount as share count (too high), causing reverts. This creates a permanent DoS on all order fills for pools with accrued interest.
threats.investigation_rationale: _calculateRepaySize declares returns as (uint repayAmt, uint repayShares). The call site assigns to (repayShares, repayAmt) = _calculateRepaySize(...). Named returns follow declaration order, so position 0 (repayAmt) maps to caller's repayShares and position 1 (repayShares) maps to caller's repayAmt — completely reversed.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _calculateRepaySize returns `(uint repayAmt, uint repayShares)` at lines 413-425; call site at line 377 destructures as `(repayShares, repayAmt) = _calculateRepaySize(...)` — positions are swapped

preconditions: Any lending pool has accrued interest (debtShareToAmt ratio > 1:1)
At least one active margin order exists
Order trigger price conditions are met

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Return value destructuring mismatch between function declaration and call site
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: repayShares gets token amount, repayAmt gets share count; fillOrder uses repayAmt (shares) for safeTransferFrom and repayShares (amount) for CORE.repay
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No validation that repayShares < totalDebtShares before calling repay

analysis_questions: What happens when CORE.repay is called with amount-as-shares that exceeds actual debt shares?
For fresh pools with 1:1 ratio, does the bug remain hidden?
Are amtOut calculations also corrupted by using the wrong repayAmt?
validation_requirements: Trace return values from _calculateRepaySize through fillOrder's safeTransferFrom and CORE.repay calls
Test fillOrder on a pool with >0 accrued interest
Verify revert occurs in PosManager.updatePosDebtShares when subtracting more shares than exist

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Any attempt to fill a margin order on a pool with accrued interest will fail because: (1) executor transfers too few borrow tokens (share count instead of amount), (2) repay is called with token amount as shares (exceeds position's actual debt shares), causing underflow revert. All stop-loss orders become non-executable, exposing users to unlimited downside.
prerequisites: Interest accrued on any borrow pool used in margin positions

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: All stop-loss and take-profit orders permanently non-executable; users lose downside protection
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users with active margin orders
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade to fix the return value ordering

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing integration test for fillOrder with non-trivial debt-to-share ratio

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: N/A — no attacker needed, this is a bug
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Bug manifests as soon as any interest accrues

threats.title: fillOrder Auto-Cancel Only Modifies Memory Copy, Order Remains Active in Storage
threats.hypothesis_description: In fillOrder, when position collateral is 0, the function loads the order into memory (`Order memory order = __orders[_orderId]`), sets the memory copy's status to Cancelled, emits CancelOrder event, and returns. However, `__orders[_orderId]` in storage remains Active. The order can be repeatedly called (wasting executor gas) and if collateral is later re-added, the stale order becomes fillable with outdated parameters.
threats.investigation_rationale: The order is loaded as `Order memory order` at line 259. The auto-cancel at line 268 modifies `order.status` (memory). The storage write `__orders[_orderId].status = OrderStatus.Filled` only occurs at the end (line 283), which is never reached in the early-return path.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Line 259: `Order memory order = __orders[_orderId]`; Line 268: `order.status = OrderStatus.Cancelled` modifies memory only; Line 269: `return` exits before storage write at line 283

preconditions: Active order exists for a position whose collateral has been removed to 0
Collateral can be re-added to the position later

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Memory-only state mutation followed by early return; storage never updated
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Order remains Active in storage despite CancelOrder event emission
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should use `__orders[_orderId].status = OrderStatus.Cancelled` instead of memory write

analysis_questions: Can collateral be re-added to a position after being fully removed?
Would the stale order parameters still be valid for the new collateral state?
Can this be combined with TECH-H001 (updateOrder without auth) for amplified impact?
validation_requirements: Verify storage state after fillOrder returns early via auto-cancel path
Test re-adding collateral and attempting to fill the 'cancelled' order
Check if repeated calls to fillOrder on the same orderId succeed without reverting

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) User's position collateral drops to 0 (through reducePos or liquidation). 2) Anyone calls fillOrder — order appears cancelled (event emitted) but storage remains Active. 3) User re-adds collateral to the position. 4) Attacker fills the stale order with potentially outdated trigger/limit prices, extracting value from the position under stale terms.
prerequisites: Position collateral reaches 0 while an order is Active

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Stale orders fillable at outdated prices when collateral is re-added; potential loss of collateral at unfavorable terms
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users who have had positions fully decollateralized then re-collateralized
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: User must manually cancelOrder after discovering the bug

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for order state after auto-cancel path in fillOrder

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Executor needs tokens to fill the order
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Requires position collateral to hit 0 then be re-added
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing emitted events against storage state

threats.title: reducePos Missing Position Existence Check Unlike Other Position Operations
threats.hypothesis_description: The reducePos function does not check `_require(initPosId != 0, Errors.POSITION_NOT_FOUND)` unlike openPos, increasePos, addCollateral, removeCollateral, and repayDebt which all perform this check. When called with a non-existent posId, initPosId defaults to 0, causing _reducePosInternal to read __marginPositions[0] (all zero values). Subsequent calls to ILendingPool(address(0)) and other zero-address contracts produce unclear reverts.
threats.investigation_rationale: Every other position-modifying function in MarginTradingHook checks initPosId != 0 before proceeding. reducePos omits this check, creating inconsistent error handling that could interact unexpectedly with position ID 0 if it were ever created.

proof_of_signal.pattern: reducePos line 195: `uint initPosId = initPosIds[msg.sender][_posId]` without subsequent `_require(initPosId != 0)` check; compare with increasePos line 109, addCollateral line 144, removeCollateral line 159, repayDebt line 172

preconditions: Caller provides a posId that doesn't exist in their mapping
Position ID 0 has somehow been created (unlikely but possible)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Missing input validation that is present in all parallel functions
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Inconsistent validation creates potential for position ID 0 interaction if it existed

analysis_questions: Can position ID 0 ever be created through PosManager?
Does the function ultimately revert due to zero-address calls, or does it silently succeed?
Could this interact with __marginPositions[0] if another user's position somehow mapped there?
validation_requirements: Call reducePos with non-existent posId and trace the revert path
Verify whether position ID 0 is creatable via keccak256 collision
Compare error messages with other functions that properly check

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user mistakenly calls reducePos with wrong posId. Instead of a clear POSITION_NOT_FOUND error, they get an opaque revert from calling functions on address(0). In the unlikely event position 0 exists (via keccak256 collision), operations could be performed on it without proper authorization.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Unclear error handling; potential unauthorized operation on position 0 if it exists

threats.test_coverage_gap: No negative test for reducePos with invalid posId

threats.title: WLpMoeMasterChef Reward Loss When First Wrapping with lpSupply Zero
threats.hypothesis_description: In WLpMoeMasterChef's updateRewards modifier, when lpSupply == 0 (no LP deposited yet), the pidAccRewardPerShares_e18 is not incremented even if rewards are distributed during the MasterChef deposit call. Any rewards distributed by MasterChef during the first deposit are permanently lost since they cannot be attributed to any LP holder.
threats.investigation_rationale: The updateRewards modifier reads lpSupply before the body (deposit) executes. For the first deposit, lpSupply == 0. After the body executes (deposit to MasterChef), reward tokens may have been distributed. The post-body loop checks `if (lpSupply != 0)` which is false for the first deposit, so rewards are never added to pidAccRewardPerShares_e18.

proof_of_signal.pattern: updateRewards modifier: line 62 `lpSupply = IMasterChef(MASTER_CHEF).getDeposit(pid, address(this))` (0 before first wrap); post-body line 71: `if (lpSupply != 0)` — false for first deposit, reward increment skipped

preconditions: First wrap call for a specific pid
MasterChef distributes rewards during the deposit call

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Reward accounting uses pre-deposit lpSupply which is 0 for first deposit
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Rewards distributed by MasterChef during first deposit are stranded in the contract
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should use post-deposit lpSupply or handle lpSupply == 0 edge case

analysis_questions: How much reward does MasterChef typically distribute during a deposit call?
Are stranded rewards permanently locked or do they get attributed on next deposit?
Can an attacker front-run to be the first depositor and then claim disproportionate rewards?
validation_requirements: Test first wrap for a pid with active reward distribution
Verify reward token balance increase in contract vs pidAccRewardPerShares update
Check if stranded rewards are eventually distributed to subsequent depositors

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: First user wraps LP for a pid. MasterChef distributes MOE rewards during the deposit. Those rewards are received by WLpMoeMasterChef but pidAccRewardPerShares_e18 is not updated (lpSupply was 0). Rewards remain in the contract. On the next deposit/withdrawal, the accumulated rewards are attributed to the current lpSupply, benefiting the second depositor disproportionately.
prerequisites: Being the first to wrap LP for a specific MasterChef pid

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Small reward token loss per first deposit per pid; accumulated rewards may benefit later depositors
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: First depositor loses their proportional rewards
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Lost rewards remain in contract and may be distributed to later users

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for first wrap behavior with reward distribution

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Only on first deposit per pid
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Reward token balance discrepancy vs accounted rewards

threats.title: InitCore.callback permits arbitrary external call execution (restricted only by _to!=this), enabling asset/approval abuse during protocol flows
threats.hypothesis_description: InitCore.callback() is publicly callable and will invoke ICallbackReceiver(_to).coreCallback{value:_value}(msg.sender,_data) for any _to other than itself. While MarginTradingHook.coreCallback checks msg.sender==CORE and _sender==address(this), other potential callback receivers (current or future) may not validate _sender similarly. This provides a generic arbitrary external call primitive from InitCore context that can be invoked inside multicall batches.
threats.investigation_rationale: Even if current receivers are safe, callback is a powerful and commonly abused primitive. It can become exploitable if any callback receiver trusts _sender (passed as msg.sender from InitCore) or uses InitCore-held allowances/balances. Also relevant for future upgrades/hooks added later.
deployment_assumptions: At least one deployed ICallbackReceiver trusts `_sender` without strict validation (e.g., assumes it is the core-authorized hook) OR holds assets/approvals that can be abused when called by InitCore.callback
InitCore holds token balances or has standing approvals that the receiver can pull during the callback
threats.config_evidence: No allowlist or access control in InitCore.callback in InitCore.sol; receiver safety depends on which callback receivers are deployed/used.

proof_of_signal.pattern: public callback with only check `_to != address(this)`; external call to arbitrary `_to` with attacker-controlled calldata/value

preconditions: Attacker can call InitCore.callback directly or via InitCore.multicall delegatecalls
A callback receiver exists that mishandles `_sender` or trusts caller (InitCore) implicitly
Receiver performs sensitive operations (token transfers, approvals, state changes) based on `_sender` or caller

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: arbitrary external call hook without allowlist/access control
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: enables invoking any ICallbackReceiver with arbitrary data/value and forged `_sender` (as perceived by receiver)
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No allowlist of trusted receivers; no restriction to calls initiated by specific internal flows

analysis_questions: Are there any other ICallbackReceiver implementations besides MarginTradingHook, and do they enforce `_sender`/caller checks?
Can callback be used inside multicall to temporarily bypass health checks by performing off-core actions that affect oracle/collateral valuation mid-tx?
Does InitCore ever hold funds/approvals that can be drained via a malicious receiver called through callback?
validation_requirements: Enumerate all deployed/upgradeable callback receivers and review their coreCallback authorization logic
Search codebase for `coreCallback(` implementations and verify checks on msg.sender and `_sender`
Simulate an attacker-deployed receiver contract called via InitCore.callback to test what can be influenced

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker calls InitCore.callback to invoke a permissive receiver (or a future upgraded hook) that interprets `_sender` as trusted and transfers tokens/changes position state based on that, potentially draining assets or corrupting accounting.
prerequisites: Presence of at least one vulnerable callback receiver integration

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potential asset theft from contracts that rely on callback trust; potential state corruption across hooked strategies
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users of any hooked strategy relying on callback receivers
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: May require disabling callback usage, patching receivers, or adding allowlist at core

threats.test_coverage_gap: Likely missing adversarial tests calling InitCore.callback to arbitrary receivers

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor for InitCore.callback calls to unknown receivers

threats.title: InitCore.transferToken is an unprotected token mover that can be abused to route funds to attacker-controlled addresses in composable flows
threats.hypothesis_description: InitCore.transferToken() is public and moves tokens from msg.sender to an arbitrary `_to`. While it requires msg.sender allowance, its existence enables composable call bundles where users or integrated contracts may be induced to call InitCore.transferToken as part of a multicall sequence, allowing subtle fund redirection (especially when combined with delegatecall-based multicall patterns and complex UIs/routers).
threats.investigation_rationale: This function expands attack surface: it is a generic token transfer primitive inside the protocol's core, callable via multicall delegatecalls. Even without phishing, other on-chain contracts can incorporate it to perform token movements that users did not intend when interacting with higher-level hooks/routers.
deployment_assumptions: A production integration/strategy contract uses InitCore.multicall with user-supplied calldata and fails to validate the included calls (e.g., allows arbitrary selectors including transferToken)
Users approve InitCore for tokens and interact through an untrusted aggregator contract
threats.config_evidence: InitCore.transferToken is public and nonReentrant without access control (InitCore.sol). Whether it is reachable in practice depends on integrator validation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: public nonReentrant function that does IERC20(_token).safeTransferFrom(msg.sender, _to, _amt) without any auth/allowlist

preconditions: User has approved InitCore to spend `_token`
Caller can cause transferToken to be invoked as part of a transaction the user initiates (e.g., via integrator contract that forwards multicall data)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: generic token transfer primitive inside core
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: can be embedded into multicall batches if an integrator permits user-supplied call data
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No selector allowlist / no restriction to only-hook callers

analysis_questions: Is InitCore.multicall intended to be user-supplied raw calldata, or do frontends/routers restrict selectors?
Do any hooks/strategies pass through arbitrary multicall arrays from users to InitCore?
Are there recommended user approvals to InitCore that increase blast radius?
validation_requirements: Search repository for any contract that forwards user calldata into InitCore.multicall
Review UI/backend patterns (if available) for selector filtering
Create a PoC integrator that forwards arbitrary multicall data and demonstrate theft using transferToken

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker deploys a contract that offers a legitimate-looking action but internally calls InitCore.multicall including transferToken to move the user's approved tokens to attacker.
prerequisites: A permissive integrator exists OR users directly call multicall with unreviewed data

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss limited to user-approved token allowances to InitCore

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests asserting multicall selector allowlists / restricted call sets

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor for multicalls that include selector of transferToken

threats.title: WLpMoeMasterChef.getPendingRewards can revert/divide-by-zero when lpSupply==0, blocking dependent state-changing flows that rely on it
threats.hypothesis_description: getPendingRewards() computes `moeRewards[0] * 1e18 / lpSupply` and `extraRewards[0] * 1e18 / lpSupply` without guarding lpSupply==0. While getPendingRewards is view, if any state-changing path (on-chain keepers, liquidation valuation, collateral checks, or hook UIs embedded on-chain) depends on it and bubbles the revert, it can block operations for pids with zero deposits.
threats.investigation_rationale: Even though it's view, in DeFi systems view functions are sometimes called by other contracts for validation. This is a concrete code defect (division-by-zero) worth checking for propagation into critical tx paths (e.g., if a wrapper/oracle uses it during liquidation/health computation elsewhere).
deployment_assumptions: Some production contract calls WLpMoeMasterChef.getPendingRewards on-chain inside a state-changing function (not shown in provided code)
There exist pids where IMasterChef.getDeposit(pid,this)==0 while getPendingRewards is queried
threats.config_evidence: No evidence in provided contracts that getPendingRewards is used internally; risk depends on external integrations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: computes `... / lpSupply` without checking lpSupply!=0

preconditions: lpSupply == 0 for the pid corresponding to _id (IMasterChef.getDeposit(pid, address(this)) == 0)
Caller invokes getPendingRewards (directly or indirectly via another contract)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: division by lpSupply without zero guard
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No if(lpSupply==0) branch to return zeros

analysis_questions: Is getPendingRewards used by any on-chain contract (oracle, hook, liquidation helper) in production?
Can an attacker force lpSupply to 0 for an existing id (e.g., by fully withdrawing) and then trigger a dependent action that calls getPendingRewards?
Should pending rewards be defined as 0 when lpSupply==0?
validation_requirements: Search codebase for on-chain calls to getPendingRewards
Simulate a pid with lpSupply==0 and call getPendingRewards to confirm revert
If used on-chain, add a guard or return zeros to prevent propagation

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker ensures a pid has zero deposits (or targets a newly added pid) and triggers an on-chain flow that queries getPendingRewards, causing it to revert and blocking that operation.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Primarily operational; could block liquidations/withdrawals if integrated on-chain
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users of wrapped LP collateral/reward tracking integrations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Code patch; off-chain callers can handle revert

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for pid with lpSupply==0 in getPendingRewards

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor reverts from getPendingRewards with division-by-zero

threats.title: LendingPool.decimals can revert for non-standard underlying tokens, cascading into share accounting failures
threats.hypothesis_description: LendingPool.decimals() calls IERC20Metadata(underlyingToken).decimals() and adds VIRTUAL_SHARE_DECIMALS. If underlyingToken does not implement decimals() or reverts, then any on-chain consumer querying pool decimals (including other contracts, DEXes, or collateral/oracle adapters) may revert. While not directly used in core arithmetic here, it can break integrations and potentially collateral valuation paths if decimals are queried on-chain elsewhere.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a practical integration hazard: some ERC20s are non-standard. If a pool is configured with such a token, the share token may become non-integrable and could block operations in other protocols that require decimals().
deployment_assumptions: A LendingPool is deployed with an underlyingToken that does not implement IERC20Metadata.decimals() correctly (reverts or returns unexpected values)
Critical protocol/integration path depends on decimals() call success
threats.config_evidence: LendingPool.initialize sets underlyingToken without validation; decimals() assumes IERC20Metadata compliance.

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC20Metadata(underlyingToken).decimals() external call without try/catch or validation

preconditions: Underlying token decimals() call reverts or is non-standard
Caller/integration queries LendingPool.decimals() (on-chain or off-chain)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: revert can break integrations; incorrect decimals can confuse accounting in external systems
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No validation during initialize; no fallback decimals

analysis_questions: Does protocol restrict underlying tokens to a vetted list with standard decimals behavior?
Are any on-chain components relying on decimals() for collateral valuation or share math?
What happens if decimals() returns extremely large values (overflow in external consumers)?
validation_requirements: Review deployment configuration for underlyingToken whitelist/standards
Test with a mock ERC20 without decimals() to observe behavior in integrations
Confirm core accounting does not rely on decimals() anywhere else

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If misconfigured with a non-standard token, integrations or wrappers interacting with the pool's share token revert on decimals(), causing operational DoS and potentially trapping user funds in external protocols.
prerequisites: Misconfiguration or addition of non-standard underlying token

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potentially material via integration breakage/trapped funds in third-party protocols
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users interacting through integrations that depend on decimals() success
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Redeploy pool or upgrade with defensive decimals handling

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests for non-IERC20Metadata underlying tokens

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Watch for decimals() call reverts in indexing/integrations

threats.title: PosManager.claimPendingRewards lacks reentrancy protection around external token transfers, enabling callback-based griefing via malicious reward tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: claimPendingRewards iterates tokens and transfers ERC20 rewards to `_to` after setting pendingRewards to 0. If a reward token is malicious (ERC777-style hooks, or ERC20 with callback on transfer), it may reenter into PosManager or other protocol contracts. While onlyAuthorized gates most state changes, reentrancy could be used to create unexpected call sequences or repeatedly revert to block claims (griefing).
threats.investigation_rationale: Although standard ERC20s should be safe, reward tokens are external and can be arbitrary depending on WLp rewarder configuration. This warrants checking for any external-call-then-state patterns (here state is updated before transfer, which is good) and whether any callable functions during reentrancy can cause inconsistent accounting or lock funds.
deployment_assumptions: A malicious/non-standard reward token is added as a reward token in WLpMoeMasterChef harvest flow and accumulated into PosManager.pendingRewards
That token's transfer triggers reentrant calls that can affect PosManager/protocol behavior
threats.config_evidence: Reward token set depends on external MasterChef reward tokens; PosManager assumes SafeERC20 transfer is non-reentrant.

proof_of_signal.pattern: external token transfer in a loop without nonReentrant; potential callback/reentrancy surface via malicious ERC20

preconditions: pendingRewards[_posId][token] > 0 for at least one token
At least one token implements a reentrant callback behavior on transfer
Reentered call reaches a function that changes relevant state or causes revert loops

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: external calls (safeTransfer) inside loop without reentrancy guard
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: malicious reward token can reenter and grief/block reward claiming or interact unexpectedly with authorization flows

analysis_questions: Can a malicious token reenter into claimPendingRewards again (same posId) and cause inconsistent behavior?
Are there any external functions callable by the token contract during reentrancy that could move collateral/debt or modify viewer mappings?
Does SafeERC20 handle ERC777 hooks safely in this context?

Map all public/external functions in PosManager callable during reentrancy and their effects
Assess whether adding ReentrancyGuardUpgradeable to PosManager is feasible in upgrade path

prerequisites: Non-standard/malicious reward token in reward set

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Primarily griefing/DoS of reward withdrawals; potential secondary impacts if reentrancy reaches other sensitive paths
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Positions with pending rewards in the malicious token
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Possible to blacklist token off-chain; on-chain fix requires upgrade

threats.title: CloseExactOut Strict Equality Check Enables 1-Wei Griefing DoS on reducePos
threats.hypothesis_description: In coreCallback's CloseExactOut path, a strict equality check `_require(IERC20(swapInfo.tokenOut).balanceOf(address(this)) == swapInfo.amtOut, Errors.SLIPPAGE_CONTROL)` is used. An attacker can front-run any reducePos transaction by sending 1 wei of the borrow token (tokenOut) to the MarginTradingHook contract, causing the balance to be swapInfo.amtOut + 1, failing the equality check and reverting the user's transaction.
threats.investigation_rationale: The strict equality (==) instead of (>=) creates a fragile check that can be trivially broken by anyone sending dust tokens to the contract. This is visible in the coreCallback function.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Line 328: '_require(IERC20(swapInfo.tokenOut).balanceOf(address(this)) == swapInfo.amtOut, Errors.SLIPPAGE_CONTROL)' uses == instead of >=. Any dust token deposit breaks this equality.

preconditions: User attempts reducePos with tokenOut == collToken (CloseExactOut swap type)

threats.title: Underwater Positions (health=0) Cannot Be Liquidated Due to Zero Incentive Multiplier
threats.hypothesis_description: LiqIncentiveCalculator.getLiqIncentiveMultiplier_e18 returns 0 when health factor is exactly 0. In InitCore.liquidate, this causes repayAmtWithLiqIncentive = repayAmt * 0 / 1e18 = 0, meaning the liquidator repays debt but receives 0 collateral. No rational liquidator will execute this, causing permanent bad debt accumulation. The health==0 bypass of post-liquidation health check further confirms the special-case handling, but without incentive, positions with health=0 are economically un-liquidatable.
threats.investigation_rationale: The early return of 0 for health_e18==0 was likely intended to avoid division by zero in (1e18/0 - 1), but it should return maxLiqIncentiveMultiplier instead. threats.title: LP Price Manipulation via Reserve Inflation When kLast==0
threats.hypothesis_description: WLpMoeMasterChef.calculatePrice_e36 uses live AMM reserves when kLast==0 via the formula 2*sqrt(r0*p0)*sqrt(r1*p1)/totalSupply. While this formula is theoretically manipulation-resistant for single swaps (k=r0*r1 stays constant), an attacker can perform multiple round-trip swaps via flash loan to accumulate swap fees, increasing k and thus inflating the LP price. Each round-trip increases k by the fee percentage (~0.3%). The inflated LP price enables excessive borrowing against WLp collateral.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code explicitly handles kLast==0 with reserve-based pricing instead of kLast-based pricing. On Merchant Moe, kLast may be 0 if the protocol fee switch is off, making this the active pricing path.
deployment_assumptions: Merchant Moe pair has kLast == 0 (protocol fee switch off)
LP tokens from such pairs are used as collateral via WLpMoeMasterChef
threats.config_evidence: WLpMoeMasterChef.sol lines 136-150 show both kLast==0 and kLast!=0 paths; actual kLast depends on Moe protocol governance

proof_of_signal.pattern: kLast==0 branch uses live reserves: 'price = 2 * (r0 * prices_e36[0]).sqrt() * (r1 * prices_e36[1]).sqrt() / totalSupply' where r0, r1 are current manipulable reserves

preconditions: kLast == 0 on the target Moe pair (fee switch off)
WLp tokens from this pair are used as collateral
Flash loan liquidity available for round-trip swaps
Pool is shallow enough for meaningful k inflation

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Flash loan → multiple round-trip swaps in pair → k increases → LP price inflates → borrow against inflated collateral → repay flash loan
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Extra borrowing = LP_value * collFactor * price_inflation. For $1M LP with 0.8 collFactor and 3% inflation: ~$24K extra borrowing

threats.title: Multiple Orders Over-Commit Collateral Leading to Unexpected Proportional Scaling
threats.hypothesis_description: When creating orders via _createOrder, individual order collAmt is checked against current position collateral, but the aggregate collAmt across all active orders for a position is never validated. A user can create multiple orders whose total collAmt exceeds their actual collateral. When orders are filled sequentially, _calculateRepaySize scales down later orders proportionally, but the effective execution price diverges from the user's intended limitPrice, potentially resulting in economic loss for the position holder.
threats.investigation_rationale: Each _createOrder checks `_collAmt <= collAmt` individually. Two orders each requesting 80% of collateral would both pass but represent 160% total commitment.

No aggregate check across __posOrderIds[initPosId]. _calculateRepaySize at line 438: 'if (_order.collAmt > totalCollAmt) _order.collAmt = totalCollAmt' silently scales down.

threats.title: Oracle Deviation Default of 0 Creates DoS When Secondary Source Is Configured
threats.hypothesis_description: In InitOracle.getPrice_e36, when both primary and secondary sources return valid prices, a deviation check is performed: (maxPrice * 1e18) / minPrice <= maxPriceDeviations_e18[token]. If maxPriceDeviations_e18 is not set (defaults to 0), this check always fails since any valid price ratio exceeds 0. This means configuring a secondary oracle source without also setting maxPriceDeviation bricks the oracle for that token, blocking all operations including liquidations.
threats.investigation_rationale: The default value of 0 in Solidity mappings creates a trap: adding a secondary source for resilience actually breaks the oracle if deviation threshold is forgotten.

threats.title: Flash Loan Feeless Borrowing Amplifies All Oracle/Price-Dependent Exploits

threats.title: LP Price Calculation Overflow for Large Pools
threats.hypothesis_description: In `WLpMoeMasterChef`, when `kLast != 0`, the LP price is calculated using `kLast.mulDiv(prices_e36[0].mulDiv(prices_e36[1], totalSupply), totalSupply)`. The intermediate multiplication `prices_e36[0] * prices_e36[1]` scales to ~1e60. When multiplied by `kLast` (which is `r0 * r1` and can easily exceed 1e40 for large pools), the value exceeds `type(uint256).max` (~1.15e77), causing an overflow revert and breaking price feeds for the LP token.
threats.investigation_rationale: Mathematical overflows in oracle price calculations cause systemic DoS for all positions utilizing the affected asset. The formula multiplies three large numbers (`kLast`, `p0`, `p1`) before dividing, creating a severe overflow bottleneck for deep liquidity pools.
deployment_assumptions: Protocol integrates with a Merchant Moe pair that has deep liquidity (e.g., >100M TVL) and kLast tracking enabled

threats.title: Front-running collateralize Allows Theft of Orphaned Deposits
threats.hypothesis_description: The `addCollateral` function in `PosManager` calculates `amtIn` based on the difference between the current token balance and the cached `__collBalances`. threats.title: WLpMoeMasterChef wrap uses safeApprove without reset; can revert for nonstandard ERC20 LP tokens causing operational DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: WLpMoeMasterChef.wrap calls `IERC20(_lp).safeApprove(MASTER_CHEF, _amt)` each time. threats.title: WLpMoeMasterChef Reward Accounting Underflow When Extra Reward Token Equals LP Token
threats.hypothesis_description: In the updateRewards modifier, balance deltas are computed as (balanceAfter - rewardBefore). During a deposit via _depositToMasterChef, LP tokens are transferred to MasterChef, reducing the LP balance. If the extraRewardToken returned by MasterChef happens to be the same as the LP token, the rewardBeforeAmts captures the LP balance INCLUDING the LP being deposited. After the deposit body executes, the LP balance drops (tokens sent to MasterChef). The subtraction (balanceAfter - rewardBefore) underflows, causing a revert. This permanently DoSes all wrap/unwrap/harvest operations for that pid.
threats.investigation_rationale: The updateRewards modifier assumes reward token balances only increase during MasterChef interactions. This invariant breaks when the reward token is the LP token itself, as deposits DECREASE the LP balance.

threats.title: LendingPool Rounding in repay Can Create Phantom Debt Shares Breaking Debt/Share Correlation Invariant
threats.hypothesis_description: In LendingPool.repay, the amount to repay is calculated with Rounding.Up: amt = _shares.mulDiv(_totalDebt, _totalDebtShares, Up). This means each partial repay over-pays slightly relative to shares destroyed. Over many small partial repays, totalDebt can reach 0 (via the safety check totalDebt > amt ? totalDebt - amt : 0) while totalDebtShares is still > 0. These stale phantom shares then distort future borrow share calculations: new borrows enter the shares = _amt branch (since totalDebt == 0) but totalDebtShares is non-zero, inflating the denominator.
threats.investigation_rationale: This breaks the fundamental invariant that (totalDebt == 0) iff (totalDebtShares == 0). While each instance creates only wei-level distortion, the cumulative effect across many pools and positions could compound.

proof_of_signal.pattern: totalDebt = _totalDebt > amt ? _totalDebt - amt : 0 can set totalDebt=0 while totalDebtShares = _totalDebtShares - _shares still > 0 due to rounding Up in amt calculation

preconditions: Multiple partial repays with small shares occur

threats.title: MarginTradingHook._transmitTokenIn Allows Overpayment Without Refund When tokenIn Is Non-WNATIVE
threats.hypothesis_description: In _transmitTokenIn, when msg.value > 0 and _tokenIn != WNATIVE, the function reverts with NOT_WNATIVE. However, in functions using both depositNative and _transmitTokenIn (openPos, increasePos, repayDebt), the depositNative modifier wraps msg.value to WNATIVE BEFORE _transmitTokenIn runs. If the user sends ETH (msg.value > 0) but _tokenIn is not WNATIVE, the depositNative modifier already wrapped the ETH to WNATIVE. Then _transmitTokenIn checks msg.value (still non-zero) and requires _tokenIn == WNATIVE, causing a revert. The already-wrapped WNATIVE from depositNative is refunded by refundNative. This is correct behavior, but the error message is misleading — user loses gas but not funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: The modifier ordering (depositNative runs first, then _transmitTokenIn) means ETH is wrapped before validation. While the refundNative modifier ultimately returns funds, the gas cost of the failed transaction is wasted. More critically, this interaction between modifier ordering and function logic could be fragile if code is modified.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _transmitTokenIn checks msg.value != 0 after depositNative already consumed the ETH — ETH is wrapped to WNATIVE but _tokenIn validation against WNATIVE may cause revert

threats.title: InitCore.setPosMode Allows Atomically Switching Between Modes With Different Risk Parameters During Multicall
threats.hypothesis_description: A user can batch operations in a multicall to borrow in mode A (with higher debt ceiling or favorable borrow factors), then switch to mode B (with more favorable collateral factors) before the end-of-multicall health check. The health check only validates the FINAL state. If mode B has higher collateral factors but was designed with lower debt ceilings, the user bypasses mode B's debt ceiling by borrowing under mode A first. The setPosMode function moves debt shares between modes in RiskManager, checking mode B's ceiling only for the transferred debt — but the debt was already created under mode A's ceiling.
threats.investigation_rationale: The multicall pattern defers health checks and allows intermediate states that wouldn't pass individual checks. Mode switching during multicall could exploit differences in risk parameters between modes.

threats.title: Multicall.getRevertMessage uses non-standard memory manipulation that may mis-decode revert data and obscure failure handling
threats.hypothesis_description: Multicall.getRevertMessage attempts to decode revert strings by manually padding and modifying bytes offsets (setting paddedData[63]=0x40). This is unusual and may mis-handle certain revert payloads (custom errors, panic codes, short data), potentially causing multicall to revert with 'MC' and obscure underlying errors. While not a direct fund-loss vector, it can impede operational debugging and, in edge cases, cause unexpected reverts if decoding logic itself fails under specific calldata patterns.
threats.investigation_rationale: Given multicall is a core execution primitive used by MarginTradingHook and MoneyMarketHook, robustness of revert propagation matters. Incorrect decoding could hinder monitoring/automation and complicate incident response; also could be used to force generic reverts ('MC') to hide exploit traces in bundled calls.

threats.title: MoneyMarketHook Leftover WETH/ETH Sweepable by Any Caller via returnNative Flag
threats.hypothesis_description: If a user calls MoneyMarketHook.execute with returnNative=false and overpays msg.value during a WNATIVE repay operation (where repayAmt < msg.value), excess WETH remains in the hook contract. Any subsequent caller can invoke execute with empty parameters and returnNative=true to sweep all WETH/ETH held by the hook, stealing the leftover funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: In _handleRepay, WNATIVE deposits msg.value then reduces repayAmt by msg.value (or sets to 0 if msg.value > repayAmt). The excess stays as WETH in the hook. The execute function's returnNative path sweeps all WETH balance and all ETH balance to msg.sender, regardless of ownership. The hook has no per-user accounting for native token balances.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _handleRepay deposits msg.value as WETH but only uses repayAmt; execute returnNative sweeps entire WETH+ETH balance to msg.sender

preconditions: User overpays msg.value in a WNATIVE repay operation with returnNative=false
MEV bot or attacker monitors for WETH balance in the hook

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: returnNative flag sweeps entire balance without tracking ownership
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: From the moment excess WETH is left until it's swept

operational_questions: How likely is overpayment in practice?
Can MEV bots atomically backrun overpayment transactions?
Is there any other mechanism that clears hook WETH balance?
validation_requirements: Send execute with WNATIVE repay and msg.value > repayAmt, returnNative=false
Verify WETH remains in hook
Call execute with empty params and returnNative=true from different address, verify sweep

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor mempool for MoneyMarketHook.execute calls with msg.value > 0 and returnNative=false
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Backrun the victim's transaction with execute({posId:0, mode:1, viewer:self, ..., returnNative:true})

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Loss of user funds left in the hook
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Minor - affects only users who overpay

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: WETH balance accumulation in MoneyMarketHook contract
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: execute calls with empty params and returnNative=true
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Backrun transactions after WNATIVE repay operations

threats.title: InitCore.multicall may leave uncheckedPosIds uncleared if any health-check loop iteration reverts after partial removals, causing persistent DoS on future multicalls
threats.hypothesis_description: InitCore.multicall sets isMulticallTx=true, executes delegatecalls, then iterates uncheckedPosIds.values() and for each posId checks health and removes it. If any _isPosHealthy(posId) path reverts due to oracle failure (e.g., price feed reverts) or external call issues while some removals already happened, the entire transaction reverts (so state rollback should restore removals). However, if any external call inside getPosHealthCurrent_e18 has side effects in external contracts (e.g., non-view price oracle or pool accrue) and causes partial state changes that persist despite revert via try/catch patterns elsewhere, it could create unexpected stuck states or repeated revert loops, effectively DoSing multicall usage.
threats.investigation_rationale: While EVM reverts roll back state in InitCore, external calls to other contracts during health checks might have non-reverting side effects if they use try/catch internally or write state and do not revert. The health check itself calls into oracle sources and pools (accrue functions in LendingPool via toAmtCurrent/debtShareToAmtCurrent), which update state. A failing oracle could cause recurring failure that blocks multicall and any hook relying on it.
deployment_assumptions: Oracle source contracts used by InitOracle perform stateful operations or revert intermittently
A token price becomes unavailable (NO_VALID_SOURCE / TOO_MUCH_DEVIATION / stale) during multicall completion
System relies on multicall for critical user operations (margin open/close)
threats.config_evidence: InitOracle.getPrice_e36 can revert on deviation/no valid sources; lending pool conversions accrue interest (stateful). No operational fallback shown.

proof_of_signal.pattern: post-multicall loop calls _isPosHealthy -> getPosHealthCurrent_e18 which calls oracle and pool accrue paths; any revert blocks multicall completion

preconditions: A multicall operation reaches completion phase (after delegatecalls)
At least one posId is in uncheckedPosIds
A downstream call inside health computation reverts or becomes unavailable (oracle/pool)
The protocol requires multicall for core workflows (e.g., MarginTradingHook uses IMulticall(CORE).multicall)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: critical finalization depends on external calls (oracle/pool accrual) that may revert
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: multicall becomes unusable during oracle outages, potentially locking key operations
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No graceful degradation path; no per-posId try/catch to isolate failures; no emergency bypass for users

analysis_questions: Can oracle or pool calls during health computation have non-reverting side effects leading to inconsistent state across retries?
What operations are blocked if multicall is unavailable (margin open/close)?
Is there an alternative path that does not require multicall for user exits?
validation_requirements: Simulate oracle reverting during the final health-check loop and assess user impact
Trace all external calls in getCollateralCreditCurrent_e36/getBorrowCreditCurrent_e36 for state mutations
Evaluate whether multicall lock (isMulticallTx) can ever be stuck true (should revert roll back) in proxy context

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker (or external market event) causes oracle sources to revert for a collateral token; any multicall that touches positions using that token cannot finalize, blocking margin operations and potentially leading to forced liquidations elsewhere.
prerequisites: Oracle outage / deviation condition; not necessarily attacker-controlled

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Operational DoS; could indirectly cause losses due to inability to manage positions during volatility
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users relying on multicall-based hooks
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Operational (fix oracle/config) or code change (fallback pricing / per-token isolation)

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests for multicall finalization under oracle revert/outage scenarios

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Track multicall reverts with oracle error codes (#700/#701/#707/#708)

threats.title: Misconfigured Minimum Liquidation Incentive Bypasses Maximum Cap
threats.hypothesis_description: In `LiqIncentiveCalculator`, the final multiplier is calculated as `Math.max(multiplier_e18, minLiqIncentiveMultiplier_e18[_mode])`, which is applied *after* capping the multiplier at `maxLiqIncentiveMultiplier_e18`. If governance misconfigures the minimum multiplier to be greater than the maximum cap, the minimum will override the maximum. This allows liquidators to extract more collateral than the protocol intended, potentially draining user positions excessively during liquidation.
threats.investigation_rationale: The order of operations in bounding the multiplier allows a configuration error to silently bypass intended safety limits.
deployment_assumptions: Governance sets `minLiqIncentiveMultiplier_e18` > `maxLiqIncentiveMultiplier_e18`

proof_of_signal.pattern: `Math.min` applied before `Math.max`, allowing `min` to exceed `max`

preconditions: Governance misconfigures the minimum multiplier

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Order of bounding operations allows min to override max

operational_questions: Does the deployment script validate min <= max?
What is the impact on user positions if the multiplier is excessively high?
validation_requirements: Review deployment configurations and governance proposals

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Governance accidentally or maliciously sets `minLiqIncentiveMultiplier_e18` higher than `maxLiqIncentiveMultiplier_e18`
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: A position becomes eligible for liquidation
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Liquidator executes liquidation and receives an excessively high incentive multiplier
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Victim loses more collateral than expected

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - requires governance to fix the configuration

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unusually high collateral payouts during liquidation
